title: Function Scope
body:
## Definition

**Function scope** means a binding is visible throughout the entire function body where it was declared, not only inside the nested block where the declaration appears.

## Why it exists

Early JavaScript only had `var` and function-level scoping. This was simpler for engines in the 1990s but causes leakage from blocks into the whole function.

## How it works

`var` declarations are function-scoped (or global if outside any function). A `var` inside an `if` block is hoisted to the top of the enclosing function and shared across the whole function.

## Example

```js
function demo() {
  if (true) {
    var count = 1;
  }
  console.log(count); // 1 — `count` is function-scoped, not block-scoped
}
```

## Common confusion

Function scope is not the same as [block scope](concept:block-scope). `let`/`const` in a function still use block scope inside that function.

## In practice

Legacy code and interview questions about `var` in loops rely on function scope behavior. Modern code avoids `var` unless maintaining legacy bundles.
bodyLinked:
## Definition

**[Function scope](concept:function-scope)** means a [binding](concept:binding) is visible throughout the entire function body where it was declared, not only inside the nested block where the declaration appears.

## Why it exists

Early JavaScript only had [var](concept:var) and function-level scoping. [This](concept:this-binding) was simpler for engines in the 1990s but causes leakage from blocks into the whole function.

## How it works

[var](concept:var) declarations are [function-scoped](concept:function-scope) (or global if outside any function). A [var](concept:var) inside an `if` block is [hoisted](concept:hoisting) to the top of the enclosing function and shared across the whole function.

## Example

```js
function demo() {
  if (true) {
    var count = 1;
  }
  console.log(count); // 1 — `count` is function-scoped, not block-scoped
}
```

## Common confusion

[Function scope](concept:function-scope) is not the same as [block scope](concept:block-scope). [let](concept:let)/[const](concept:const) in a function still use [block scope](concept:block-scope) inside that function.

## In practice

Legacy code and interview questions about [var](concept:var) in loops rely on [function scope](concept:function-scope) behavior. Modern code avoids [var](concept:var) unless maintaining legacy bundles.
